summary: Temporal is not needed for the current @bears worker runtime; the local worker/state-file model plus a simple queue/lease is enough, and Dagger is only a wrapper for containerized execution.

why: keeps state in explicit repo/runtime files
fits the current closeout and policy-gate flow
does not replace durable worker state or retry history
adds orchestration overhead without solving closeout gates
prevents duplicate starts with explicit leases
keeps ownership and recovery visible in files
matches the current bounded worker model
adds durable task queues, worker polling, and retry history
needs a service and worker process boundary
is only worth adopting if the local model fails at scale

adoption_preconditions.description: The closeout path and policy gates run end-to-end without Temporal.
A real worker-runtime gap remains after the local worker/state-file model and simple queue/lease are used.
A named owner can run, observe, and retire the Temporal service and worker fleet.
Retry, routing, replay, and recovery needs are explicit enough to justify the extra service.
